text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum over the pelvic promontory, initiating the abdominoperineal resection procedure.
The surgeon utilizes pneumoperitoneum to visualize the air tracking along the posterior aspect of the superior rectal artery.
The surgeon carefully drops the hypogastric nerves from the posterior aspect of the superior rectal artery, ensuring precise manipulation to avoid nerve damage.
The surgeon identifies the back of the superior rectal artery, with a clear view of the left iliac vein expected to be visible shortly.
The assistant applies traction and counter traction to facilitate the dissection around the superior rectal artery, ensuring precise manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a monopolar scissor in a brush-like technique to gently progress traction, avoiding cutting while manipulating tissue.
The surgeon performs medial dissection along the side of the inferior mesenteric artery, meticulously exposing each anatomical layer during the procedure.

The robotic arm III provides traction under the superior rectal artery, while the assistant uses a U-hand forceps to apply counter traction, facilitating clear visualization and manipulation of the tissue.
The surgeon identifies the hypogastric nerve at the pelvic brim and continues the dissection parallel to the inferior mesenteric artery, maintaining a clear view of the anatomical structures.
As the dissection progresses, the left common iliac artery becomes visible, indicating the advancement of the surgical field towards the pelvic region.
The surgeon continues meticulous dissection around the inferior mesenteric artery, using a monopolar scissor with a print brush technique to carefully separate tissue layers.
